coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots synthesized using phenylalanine and citric acid for selective detection of Fe3+ ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Pu, Zheng-Fen
Wen, Qiu-Lin
Yang, Yan-Ju
Cui, Xiao-Miao
Ling, Jian
Liu, Peng
Cao, Qiu-E
coredata.dc:description: A facile, economical and one-step hydrothermal method was used to synthesize fluorescent carbon dots by utilizing citric acid as carbon source and phenylalanine to provide nitrogen. The as-prepared fluorescence carbon dots had strong blue light emission around 440 nm. As confirmed by UVvis absorption, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and transmission electron microscope characterization, the carbon dots were small and very stable in water for using as a fluorescent probe. It was also found that the fluorescence of the carbon dots could be quenched in the presence of Fe3+ ions, and the quenching rate was linear with the concentration of Fe3+ ions. We here proposed a static quenching mechanism about the fluorescence of the Phe-CDs could be selectively quenched by Fe3+ ions, which was because these Fe3+ ions could easily combine with the hydroxyl or carboxyl groups on the surface of Phe-CDs and induced aggregation. In addition, the pH had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of the Phe-CDs and maintained excellent fluorescence intensity even under extreme pH value conditions and could be used for the detection of Fe3+ ions. We have demonstrated that the method using the carbon dots for Fe3+ ions detection was rapid, reliable, and selective with a detection limit as low as 0.720 μM and a dynamic range from 5.0 to 500.0 μM. Moreover, the results of determination Fe3+ ions in tap water samples indicated that the presented method has potential for practical application in environmental metal analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Phenylalanine
Fluorescence assay
Fe3+ ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of Phe-CDs to Fe3+ ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectrum (A); Electron microscopy image (B–D) of Phe-CDs and High-resolution TEM images of Phe-CDs (E). Insets, photos of the Phe-CDs under natural light and UV irradiation. Experimental conditions: the concentration of citric acid was 8.0 mM; the concentration of phenylalanine was 10.0 mM; the reaction time was 30 h; and the reaction temperature was 180 °C.

UV–visible absorption spectrum (A) and infrared spectroscopy (B) of Phe-CDs. Experimental conditions: the concentration of citric acid was 8.0 mM; the concentration of phenylalanine was 10.0 mM; the reaction time was 30 h; and the reaction temperature was 180 °C.
XPS spectra of the Phe-CDs (A). High-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (B-D).

Fluorescent responses of Phe-CDs at different pH (A), and degree of quenching of Phe-CDs in the presence of 50.0 μM Fe3+ ions (B). Fluorescence intensities of Phe-CDs after adding various concentrations of NaCl solutions (C)and degree of quenching of Phe-CDs in the presence of 50.0 μM Fe3+ ions after adding various concentrations of NaCl solutions (D). Experimental conditions: the concentration of Phe-CDs was 3.0 mM (calculated by the concentration of phenylalanine); BR buffer, 20 mM.

UV–Vis absorption spectra (A) and the fluorescence lifetime decay curves (B) of the Phe-CDs in the presence of different concentration of Fe3+ ions, the average Phe-CDs and Phe-CDs/Fe3+ fluorescent lifetime were 3.60 ns. Electron microscopy image of Phe-CDs before (C) and after (D) addition of 50.0 μM Fe3+ ions.

Selectivity of Phe-CDs for metal ions (A) and interference of other metals ions for Fe3+ ions detection (B). Experimental conditions: the concentration of Phe-CDs was 3.0 mM (calculated by the concentration of phenylalanine); the concentration of Fe3+ ions were 30.0 μM, and the concentration of other metal ions were 90.0 μM; NaAC-HAC buffer,20 mM, pH, 5.0; reaction time, 30 min.

The fluorescence quenching of the Phe-CDs by Fe3+ ions (A) and a linear relationship between quenching ratio and concentration of Fe3+ ions. Experimental conditions: the concentration of Phe-CDs was 3.0 mM (calculated by the concentration of phenylalanine); NaAC-HAC buffer,20 mM; pH, 5.0; reaction time, 30 min.
Comparison of proposed methods with other Fe3 + ions assay methods for pH range.

row.entry: Nitrogen and Sulfur co-doped carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The comparison of the proposed method with other methods of Fe3+ determination.
Detection of Fe3+ ions in actual water samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots synthesized using phenylalanine and citric acid for selective detection of Fe3+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Functional Molecules Analysis and Biotransformation Key Laboratory of Universities in Yunnan Province, School of Chemical Science and Technology, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Functional Molecules Analysis and Biotransformation Key Laboratory of Universities in Yunnan Province
School of Chemical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Functional Molecules Analysis and Biotransformation Key Laboratory of Universities in Yunnan Province, School of Chemical Science and Technology, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile, economical and one-step hydrothermal method was used to synthesize fluorescent carbon dots by utilizing citric acid as carbon source and phenylalanine to provide nitrogen. The as-prepared fluorescence carbon dots had strong blue light emission around 440 nm. As confirmed by UVvis absorption, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and transmission electron microscope characterization, the carbon dots were small and very stable in water for using as a fluorescent probe. It was also found that the fluorescence of the carbon dots could be quenched in the presence of Fe3+ ions, and the quenching rate was linear with the concentration of Fe3+ ions. We here proposed a static quenching mechanism about the fluorescence of the Phe-CDs could be selectively quenched by Fe3+ ions, which was because these Fe3+ ions could easily combine with the hydroxyl or carboxyl groups on the surface of Phe-CDs and induced aggregation. In addition, the pH had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of the Phe-CDs and maintained excellent fluorescence intensity even under extreme pH value conditions and could be used for the detection of Fe3+ ions. We have demonstrated that the method using the carbon dots for Fe3+ ions detection was rapid, reliable, and selective with a detection limit as low as 0.720 μM and a dynamic range from 5.0 to 500.0 μM. Moreover, the results of determination Fe3+ ions in tap water samples indicated that the presented method has potential for practical application in environmental metal analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), also known as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), is one of well-known fluorescent nanomaterials with spheroidal shape and particle size around 2–20 nm [1–3]. Due to their special structure and quantum size effect, dielectric confinement effect and surface effect [4], carbon dots has fluorescence with wide excitation range and narrow emission peak [5,6] and larger Stokes shift [7]. The surface of carbon dots contains rich functional groups. With the difference in raw materials, synthesis methods and post-synthesis functionalization, the functional groups of carbon dots will change and lead to different properties, such as water solubility, biocompatibility [8,9]. Fluorescent carbon dots have profound impact on the sustainable development of life sciences and information technology and basic research in the material field [10]. In recent years, they have contributed to advances in detection of metal ions [11–15], bioimaging [16,17], photocatalysis [18], light emitting devices [19], medicine [20,21] and DNA assay [22].

There are two main methods for preparing carbon quantum dots. One is prepared using large carbon materials, such as carbon fibers, graphite powder and graphene [23–28], and the another is physical or chemical treat organic compounds such as polyethylene glycol, citric acid, sucrose, glucose and chitosan [29–34]. Comprised with the method using large carbon material, chemical treat organic compounds has advantages of inexpensive and biocompatible raw materials, relatively green and mild synthetic routes and no special precision instruments are required [35]. Carbon dots prepared by organic compounds have rich hydroxyl, carboxyl, and epoxy groups stabilize the energy trap on the surface of the carbon dots and cause the fluorescence emission of carbon dots. Although the mechanism for the fluorescence of carbon dots is still unclear, the hypotheses of emission defects and electron conjugate structures are two explanations for it [36,37]. Thus, researches on the synthesis of fluorescence carbon dots using organic compounds have attracted much attention in recent year.

In this paper, phenylalanine associated with citric acid were used as carbon sources for synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots. The method was a facile, economical and one-step hydrothermal method and the as-prepared fluorescence carbon dots had strong blue light emission around 440 nm. We characterized the structure of the as-prepared CDs and studied the fluorescence property of CDs in the presence of metal ions. We found that the fluorescence of the CDs could be selectively quenched by Fe3+ ions and the fluorescence quenching rate had a good linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+ ions in the range from 5.0 to 500.0 μM. In addition, in most of the current studies, the pH had a great influence on the fluorescence intensity of most carbon dots, and the range of pH was limited when detecting Fe3+ ions and affected its selectivity [15,38–40]. However, in this study, we found that the pH had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots and maintained excellent fluorescence intensity even under extreme pH conditions and could be used for the detection of Fe3+ ions with a good selectivity. Therefore, we established a fluorescent method for selective detection of Fe3+ ions using the carbon quantum dots. The method had good accuracy, excellent selectivity and could realize the detection of Fe3+ ions under extreme pH conditions, which presented the potential practical application in environmental analysis.

Phenylalanine (AR, 99.0%) was purchased from Beijing Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Citric acid (AR, 99.5%) was purchased from Tianjin Sailboat Chemical Reagent Technology Co., Ltd. Metal salt used in laboratory, including CuSO4∙5H2O, CaCl2, ZnSO4∙7H2O, MgSO4∙7H2O, CoSO4∙7H2O, CdSO4∙8H2O, Pb(NO3)2, FeCl3∙6H2O, CrCl3∙6H2O, and SnCl2∙2H2O, etc. were all commercially available analytical reagent. Water for solution preparation was ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ) prepared by Milli-Q ultrapure water system. All reagents were directly used without further purified.

The fluorescence and absorption spectra were measured using a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) and an UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan), respectively. FT-IR spectra were measured using a Nicolet iS10 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (American Thermo Fisher Scientific). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) data were collected on a K-Alpha+ X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific). The pH values of the buffer solution were measured by PHS-25 pH meter (Shanghai Thunder Magnetic Instrument Factory, China). The fluorescent color of CDs was observed by ZF-1 three-purpose ultraviolet analyzer. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images was taken on JEM 2100 high resolution transmission electron microscope (Japan, Hitachi, Ltd.). The solutions were blended by QL-901 vortex mixer (Kylin-Bell Lab Instruments Co., Ltd., Haimen, China). The preparation of CDs used a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave (Shanghai Huotong Experimental Instrument Co., Ltd.).

The carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis, citric acid (8.0 mM) and phenylalanine (10.0 mM) were sufficiently dissolved in 25.0 mL of ultrapure water, and then the solution was heated at 180 °C for 30 h in a 50.0 mL autoclave made of poly(tetrafluoroethylene). After that, the obtained yellow product was placed in a dialysis bag (Mw = 3500 Da) to remove the unreacted reagents and the water should be changed every half hour until the water in dialysis was clear and transparent for 15 h. When CDs were used for detection, the concentration of original solution was calculated using the concentration of phenylalanine. In order to meet the needs of characterization, sometimes it needed to be prepared as a solid, and preparation method was as follows: the solution was taken out and dried in a vacuum oven at 65 °C for 3 days to obtain the solid powder products which was placed in a refrigerator at 4 °C for storage. They were used for further characterization and application. To make identification easier, the carbon dots made from phenylalanine were abbreviated as Phe-CDs.

For Fe3+ ions detection, in a typical case, 300.0 μL Phe-CDs was mixed different concentration Fe3+ ions, which was diluted to 1.0 mL with NaAC-HAC (pH = 5.0) buffer solution. After placement at room temperature for 30.0 min, the fluorescence spectra were recorded. For selectivity of Fe3+ ions, other ions including Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ were tested under the same experimental conditions. The concentration of Fe3+ ions and other metal ions was always the same. For investigating the practicality, water sample collection from laboratory tap (Yunnan University) were utilized to place ultrapure water. The assay condition was same as mentioned above for Fe3+ ions detection.

The Phe-CDs were synthesized utilizing citric acid and phenylalanine to as raw material through a hydrothermal treatment. The method was similar to some reported method with modifications [12]. As shown in Fig. 1A, the Phe-CDs showed a strong fluorescence emission with maximum emission wavelength at 440 nm and maximum excitation at 335 nm. The obvious blue fluorescence could be observed under the illumination of UV (365 nm) light in aqueous solution (the inset of Fig. 1A). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images (Fig. 1B–D) demonstrated that the Phe-CDs exhibited spherical particles with a diameter of about 2–3 nm. Moreover, the HRTEM image displayed the clear lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of approximately 0.21 nm (Fig. 1E), which represented that the Phe-CDs exhibited good crystallinity with graphitic structure [41,42]. The functional group of Phe-CDs was studied by UV–vis absorption and Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) Spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2A, the Phe-CDs has two characteristic peaks: one was a strong absorption peak at 250 nm which was generated by a π-π* transition of CO or CC. The another was at 290 nm which was a weak absorption peak and generated by the n-π* transition. In addition, as illustrated in Fig. 2B, the absorptions at 3300–2800 cm− 1 could be attributed to OH stretching vibration and NH stretching vibration, while the absorptions at 1713.05 cm− 1 confirmed the presence of CO bonds. Moreover, the absorption peak at 1318.59 cm− 1 was generated by CH bending vibration and the absorption peak at 1173.71 cm− 1 indicated the presence of CN bonds. According to the FTIR spectrum of Phe-CDs, it could be preliminarily judged presence of these functional groups such as -OH, -COOH, -NH2 and benzene ring. These results were similar to that of many other reported CDs [19,38,43], so that we could prove the as-prepared fluorescent product was typical CDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted to determine the surface composition and chemical environments of the synthesized Phe-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3A, the XPS survey spectrum of Phe-CDs showed the peaks at 286.1, 402.2 and 532.6 eV correspond to the elements of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. And the XPS spectra revealed that the Phe-CDs were mainly composed of carbon (70.35%), oxygen (21.85%) and nitrogen (7.8%). The high-resolution spectrum of C1s in Fig. 3B exhibited three main peaks. The binding energy peak at 284.8 eV was attributed to the CC bond, and the peak at about 286.7 eV could be assigned to CO and CN, while the peaks around 288.5 eV could be ascribed to CO. In addition, the high-resolution N 1s spectrum showed CH linkage (400.0 eV) and CN bond (401.2 eV) (Fig. 3C), which was in good agreement with the results shown in C 1s spectrum. The high-resolution O 1s in Fig. 3D displayed four components at 530.8, 531.4, 532.9 and 533.4 eV could be ascribed to –COOH, C–OH, CO, and H–O–H, respectively. Thus, the surface components of the Phe-CDs determined by the XPS were in good agreement with FTIR results.

To prepare a desirable fluorescent Phe-CDs, the effects of raw material concentration, reaction time and temperature on the fluorescence of the Phe-CDs were studied and the optimized experimental conditions were obtained here. As shown in Supplementary Materials Fig. S1, the strongest fluorescence intensity was obtained at the concentrations of phenylalanine was 10.0 mM and citric acid was 8.0 mM. The fluorescence response of the Phe-CDs upon the different reaction time (Supplementary Materials, Fig. S2) indicated that prolonging of the reaction time would increase the fluorescence intensity of the Phe-CDs and the fluorescence reached maximum after reacted for 30 h. The effect of reaction temperature (Supplementary Materials, Fig. S3) showed that the strongest fluorescence intensity was obtained at 180 °C. Thus, the optimization experimental conditions for Phe-CDs preparation should be at carried on 10.0 mM of phenylalanine and 8 mM of citric acid at a temperature of 180 °C for 30 h.

The photostability of Phe-CDs was very important because Phe-CDs was used for practical sensing applications, the material must be stable toward ambient environments. As shown in Supplementary Materials, Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity did not display significant change after continuous irradiation under a Xe lamp for several hours, indicating the excellent photostability of the fluorescent probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of Phe-CDs to Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Supplementary Materials Fig. S5, the fluorescence of Phe-CDs was obviously quenched efficiently in the presence of Fe3+ ions, and the quenching rate increased when the concentration of Fe3+ ions was arisen. And the effect of pH as one of the major variables altering the intensity of fluorescent species was evaluated. Fig. 4A presented the fluorescence intensities of Phe-CDs at different pH values. As could be seen that the material had strong fluorescence activities in the range of pH values from 2.0 to 11.0 and the Phe-CDs displayed stable fluorescent even under extreme pH conditions. Further, to study the effect of pH to alteration the detection performance of the CDs toward the Fe3+ ions, an additional test has been done by studying the quenching of the CDs with Fe3+ ions of the same concentration under different pH conditions (Fig. 4B). The results showed high degree of quenching in the pH values from 2.0 to 11.0, but the degree of quenching had slightly fluctuating. Additionally, the influence of pH on the selectivity of Fe3+ ions sensing against other common metal ions was also studied. As shown in Supplementary Materials, Fig. S6, the fluorescence response of the Phe-CDs upon the addition of these common metal ions in pH values from 2.0 to 11.0, was studied, respectively. When the same concentration of interfering metal ions was added into the Phe-CDs solution at different pH values, the addition of Fe3+ ions caused strong fluorescence response and excellent selectivity. The available pH range for CDs was comparable with or more excellent than most reported fluorescence analytical method for Fe3+ ions detection (Table 1 ). We also investigated the effect of ionic strength on the stability of Phe-CDs in a solution of 20.0 mM NaAC-HAC buffer (pH 5.0) containing different concentrations of NaCl. As shown in Fig. 4C and D, the fluorescence intensities remained constant with the increase of ionic strength and had almost no effect on the detection of Fe3+ ions. Taking the results above, these findings suggested that the Phe-CDs were highly stable even under extreme conditions.

We hypothesized that Fe3+ ions coordinated with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of Phe-CDs to bridge neighboring Phe-CDs together and that fluorescence quenching might occur as a result of the induced aggregation of Phe-CDs [44]. The relatively high selectivity of the Phe-CDs toward Fe3+ ions over other metal ions was attributed to the half-filled 3d orbital of Fe3+ ions and the unique interaction of Fe3+ ions. Fe3+ ions displayed the strongest fluorescence quenching effect on Phe-CDs, attributing to greater affinity and effective chelation kinetics between the Fe3+ ions and the abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of Phe-CDs, which facilitated the complex formation [45]. The UV–Vis absorption spectra showed that the Phe-CDs had a strong absorption peak at 250 nm and Fe3+ ions showed a strong absorption centered at 350 nm. With the addition of Fe3+ ions in Phe-CDs solution, the absorption peak intensity of Phe-CDs increased obviously, meanwhile, the absorption peak of Fe3+ ions disappeared, which indicated the reaction happened between Phe-CDs and Fe3+ ions (Fig. 5A).

Changes in the FTIR spectra of Phe-CDs before and after the addition of Fe3+ ions further confirmed this hypothesis (Supplementary Materials, Fig. S7). The FT-IR spectral characteristics of Phe-CDs were completely changed with the addition of Fe3+ ions, suggesting the formation of Phe-CDs/Fe3+ complex chelate. Specifically, Wide peak at approximately from 3300 to 2800 cm−1 were converted into a narrow peak at approximately 3065 cm−1 after the addition of Fe3+ ions. In addition, an extensive decrease in the intensity for the peaks at 1713.05 and 1173.71 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum confirmed the possible binding of Fe3+ ions to the −COOH and −OH groups of the Phe-CDs. Compared with the FT-IR spectrum of Phe-CDs, Phe-CDs/Fe3+ complex weakened the absorbance of C−H, CO, CC, and C−O bonds, indicating that the chemical bonds on the surface of Phe-CDs change because of the high affinity between Fe3+ ions and oxygen-containing functional groups from the Phe-CDs. As mentioned above, the fluorescence intensity quenching might be due to the formation of ferric-hydroxyl or ferric-carboxyl complexes at the surface of Phe-CDs [45].

Time-resolved fluorescence spectra of Phe-CDs (Fig. 5B) indicated that the average Phe-CDs fluorescent lifetime was 3.60 ns. No obvious change in lifetime was observed after the introduction of Fe3+ ions. In order to elucidate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, the Phe-CDs/Fe3+ complex was characterized using TEM. As shown in Fig. 5C, the Phe-CDs exhibited discrete dots and were very small in size, but the monodispersed Phe-CDs aggregated seriously in the presence of 50.0 μM Fe3+ ions, and the average size of the Phe-CDs/Fe3+ complex was needlelike particles about 100–200 nm (Fig. 5D). These results demonstrated that the synthesized Phe-CDs were capable of detecting Fe3+ ions based on aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching [38].

As the fluorescence of Phe-CDs could be quenched in the presence of Fe3+ ions, it was possible to establish a quantitative method for Fe3+ ions detection using Phe-CDs as fluorescent probe. The experimental conditions, such as reaction time and pH needed to be determined before determining the detected parameters. As illustrated in Supplementary Materials Fig. S8, after the addition of different amount of Fe3+ ions (5.0 μM, 25.0 μM, 125.0 μM) in the Phe-CDs, the fluorescence of the Phe-CDs was significantly quenched and finished within 30 min. For the pH of the reaction, most reports had suggested that the recognition mechanism results from the special coordination interaction between Fe3+ ions and the hydroxyl or carboxyl groups on the surface of the CDs, and CDs could form complexes with Fe3+ to induce static quenching [44]. Therefore, the best conditions reported in most studies were carried out under relatively acidic conditions [38], so in the next experiment, the pH value was determined to be 5.0. Under the optimized analytical conditions, Phe-CDs was used to quantitative detection of Fe3+ ions. As shown in Fig. 6A, the proposed method has high selectivity. No obvious fluorescence quenching was observed for representative cations at concentration 3 times than Fe3+ ions. Furthermore, the interference of Fe3+ ions detection in the presence of other ions was also studied and shown in Fig. 6B. The results indicated that the detection of Fe3+ ions using Phe-CDs would not be interfered in the present of 90.0 μM of other cations.

As shown in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence quenching rate of Phe-CDs had a good linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+ ions added. The calibration curve was I 0/I = 0.0062c Fe(III) + 0.74131 in the Fe3+ concentration range from 5.0 to 500.0 μM, with correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.9959 and the limit of detection (LOD, 3σ) of 0.720 μM. The analytical parameters were comparable with or even better than most reported fluorescence analytical method for Fe3+ ions detection [39,46–48] (Table 2 ).

In order to further investigated whether the method had practicality and accuracy for the detection of Fe3+ ions. We collected tap water from our laboratory to test reliability of the method by standardized addition method. As shown in Table 3 , the results indicated that the method had a recovery of 88.95–105.97% for detection of Fe3+ ions. Thus, we believed that the proposed analytical method using Phe-CDs as a fluorescent probe for detection of Fe3+ ions was a reliable method with high accuracy and selectivity.

In summary, a fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized using citric acid and phenylalanine as raw materials via a simple hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 30.0 h. The carbon dots showed a yellow transparent solution under visible light conditions and emitted blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light, and it displayed good fluorescence emission which remained highly homogeneous for a long period of time without any precipitation at room temperature. The TEM images demonstrated that the carbon dots exhibited spherical particles with a diameter of about 2–3 nm. XPS, UV–vis and FTIR spectrum indicated the presence of functional groups such as -OH, -COOH, -NH2 and benzene on their surface, the pH values had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of the Phe-CDs and maintained excellent fluorescence intensity even under extreme pH conditions and could be used for the detection of Fe3+ ions, high selectivity and sensitivity to detection of Fe3+ ions with low detection limits of 0.720 μM. Furthermore, the carbon dots were efficient and environmentally friendly and could be prepared without further chemical modification. Therefore, the carbon dots prepared here had promising applications in the detection of Fe3+ ions in environmental water samples.
Zheng-Fen Pu: Data curation, Writing- Original draft preparation, Formal analysis.
Qiu-Lin Wen: Conceptualization, Methodology.
Yan-Ju Yang: Project administration.
Xiao-Miao Cui: Investigation.
Jian Ling: Supervision, Writing - Reviewing and Editing, Visualization.
Peng Liu: Software.
Qiu-E Cao: Resources.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21565030), Program for Excellent Young Talents, Yunnan University and National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Education (Yunnan University).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.117944.

RSC Adv.

Anal Chem 2019, 91 (15), 9690-9697.
